segments.title: A campaign fleet overwhelmed by submarines
segments.summary: The updated Ultimate Admiral campaign opens with Britain holding an unwieldy thousand-ship fleet and far more submarines than the game can comfortably sustain.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke begins by reducing a submarine force that had grown to well over a thousand boats, while still retaining an enormous fleet. The campaign illustrates how a force can become administratively and financially unmanageable even when it is militarily dominant. He also notes a game-system quirk: new ships may be mothballed when crew are scarce while older vessels remain active.

evidence.note: He describes cutting the submarine fleet from roughly one thousand to 339 boats while retaining about a thousand ships.
He explains that the campaign had grown beyond what the game could handle and required submarine reductions.

segments.title: Peacetime budgets versus a wartime-scale fleet
segments.summary: A postwar funding cut leaves a huge British fleet unaffordable, showing how maintenance, training, research, and shipyard capacity compete for naval money.
segments.body: The campaign's central problem is not a shortage of ships but the cost of keeping an oversized force ready during peace. Dr. Clarke contrasts the generous wartime position with a peacetime budget that leaves him running a deficit despite Britain’s broad imperial position. The resulting trade-off is familiar to naval planning: retaining force structure can require cuts to readiness, research, or new construction.

evidence.note: He says the game’s budget system leaves the navy bankrupt after wartime funding ends.
He reviews fixed expenses, training and technology spending, shipyard capacity, and a possible research cut.

segments.title: Mothballing and scrapping to make a fleet affordable
segments.summary: The campaign turns fleet reduction into a practical exercise: retire old capital ships, place regional forces in reserve, and weigh mothball savings against active readiness.
segments.body: Facing an unsustainable deficit, Dr. Clarke selects older battleships and regional forces for scrapping or mothballing. He compares active and reserve upkeep directly, finding that a mothballed ship costs far less but gives up immediate availability. The moment makes the maintenance burden of capital ships tangible and shows why naval planners cannot treat nominal hull numbers as the same thing as usable combat power.

evidence.note: He raises tension with Japan while identifying older battleships to retire and calculates their monthly upkeep.
He compares mothballed and active costs and places ships in several regional fleets into reserve.

segments.title: Political control can frustrate naval strategy
segments.summary: Unable to declare war despite a financial incentive to do so, the campaign highlights the tension between political decision-making, readiness, and operational plans.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats the game rule preventing a unilateral declaration of war as an exaggerated version of a real strategic frustration: naval officers may prepare forces, but political authorities decide whether they fight. He keeps the Baltic force active while placing other ships in reserve, preserving a ready option against Germany while reducing costs elsewhere. The episode distinguishes peacetime economy from the readiness required for a plausible contingency.

evidence.note: He cannot declare war, raises tension instead, and keeps the Baltic fleet active for a possible German war.
He discusses normal peacetime mothballing and recovers a positive monthly balance while selling old ships.

segments.title: Could a harsher naval treaty have broken the challengers?
segments.summary: A game-driven counterfactual asks whether higher capital-ship and carrier allowances for Britain and America could have forced Japan and Italy into an unsustainable naval race.
segments.body: Using his own overwhelming in-game fleet as a prompt, Dr. Clarke proposes a deliberately unequal treaty structure. Britain and the United States would retain or build many more capital ships and carriers, while Japan and Italy would be encouraged to spend heavily to maintain a smaller ratio. It is a speculative argument rather than a historical claim, but it gives viewers a clear way to think about how treaty ratios, industrial capacity, and fiscal pressure interact.

evidence.note: He outlines a counterfactual treaty with larger British and American capital-ship and carrier allowances and a 7:10 Japanese ratio.

segments.title: Treaty limits need credible enforcement
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke argues that arms-control agreements restrain only willing members unless they operate within a system capable of detecting and punishing violations.
segments.body: The discussion moves from capital-ship ratios to the wider problem of compliance. Dr. Clarke’s point is that a domestic regulatory system can control access more reliably than an international treaty lacking universal membership and enforcement power. For naval history, the useful takeaway is not a simple verdict on any treaty but the need to ask who monitors compliance, what penalties are credible, and whether outside powers can evade the arrangement.

evidence.note: He argues that agreements depend on parties abiding by them and contrasts domestic controls with the international treaty system.
He concludes that the treaties could control only members that continued to participate.

segments.title: British shipbuilding capacity in the dreadnought race
segments.summary: A short reflection on Britain building roughly a third of the world’s dreadnoughts underscores the industrial imbalance behind the Anglo-German naval race.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke points to Britain’s large share of global dreadnought construction and to British yards that still lacked enough orders. His conclusion is that comparisons based only on headline ship totals can miss the deeper advantage of industrial depth. It is a useful prompt to examine whether a rival could sustain the same rate of capital-ship construction, repair, and replacement over time.

evidence.note: He says Britain built nearly a third of the world’s dreadnoughts and questions whether Germany could have won the naval race.

segments.title: Deterrence that prevents the battle you wanted
segments.summary: The campaign’s greatest success is also its disappointment: Britain’s strength deters every opponent, leaving no naval battle to fight.
segments.body: With 216 provinces and a much larger fleet than any rival, Dr. Clarke finds that the game’s opponents avoid war. He describes deterrence as highly successful but unsatisfying for a player seeking combat. The watch point is a compact lesson in the strategic purpose of naval power: preventing an enemy from choosing war can be an outcome in itself, even when it offers no dramatic battle narrative.

evidence.note: He says other powers are too frightened to declare war and calls deterrence very successful.

segments.title: Designing HMS Deterrence as a battlecruiser
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke turns a heavy-cruiser replacement into a 45,000-ton battlecruiser, balancing armament, range, protection, stability, and crew demand.
segments.body: The design exercise begins with a proposed battlecruiser carrying 9.2-inch main guns and long-range fire control, then works through secondary batteries, shells, bulkheads, draft, weight, and roll. The final ship, named HMS Deterrence, is described as a 45,000-ton heavy-cruiser equivalent with 15-inch guns, six-inch secondary armament, and 40 mm anti-aircraft weapons. It is a useful game-based illustration of the trade-offs that make warship design a balance rather than a search for a single maximum statistic.

evidence.note: He develops the battlecruiser’s guns, range, displacement, bulkheads, and stability characteristics.
He finalizes the design as HMS Deterrence and lists its displacement, main battery, secondary battery, and anti-aircraft armament.

segments.title: Ordering a battlecruiser class for a balanced fleet
segments.summary: The campaign closes with an order for 24 battlecruisers and a deliberately balanced future fleet of capital ships, cruisers, destroyers, and submarines.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke orders a class of 24 battlecruisers while checking the remaining yard capacity and discussing the need for balanced squadrons. He then sketches a future fleet with battleships and battlecruisers backed by light cruisers, destroyers, and submarines. Although the numbers are deliberately extravagant in the game, the discussion usefully frames force structure as a mixture of capital ships, screens, and undersea forces rather than one ship type alone.

evidence.note: He orders 24 battlecruisers, checks spare construction capacity, and lays out a desired fleet composition.
